title: The Indexing Pipeline, Stage by Stage
concept: Parse, chunk, enrich with metadata, embed, and write to the vector store as a repeatable pipeline.

exercises.prompt: What was the primary goal of the ingestion stage covered in the previous lesson?

options.text: Converting messy file formats into clean, standardized text
Building neural network models from scratch
Designing web user interfaces for chat applications

exercises.explain: Ingestion cleans raw files (like PDFs, HTML, or Word docs) and converts them into standardized, clean text before any pipeline stages run.

cards.title: From Text to Searchable Knowledge
cards.body: Once you have clean text from your documents, how does it actually get into a vector database? You don't just dump whole books in at once; you need an automated, step-by-step assembly line to process, tag, and store it.

cards.title: What is an Indexing Pipeline?
cards.body: An indexing pipeline is a systematic, step-by-step process that prepares raw documents for semantic search. It breaks files down, adds context, converts words to numbers, and stores them in a searchable database.

cards.title: The Assembly Line Analogy
cards.body: Think of the indexing pipeline like an automated assembly line in a toy factory. Raw plastic (documents) is shaped into clean blocks, painted with labels (metadata), boxed into standard sizes (chunks), barcoded (embedded), and stacked perfectly on warehouse shelves (vector store).

cards.title: Pipeline in Action
cards.body: When processing a 50-page PDF manual, the pipeline first extracts the text, splits it into 200-word paragraphs, tags each paragraph with the chapter name, creates a 1536-dimensional vector for each, and uploads them to Pinecone.

cards.title: Vector Stores Aren't Magic Dustbins
cards.body: Many believe vector stores automatically organize and chunk raw files for you. In reality, you must build the pipeline to clean, chunk, and embed the data yourself before saving it.

cards.title: Pipeline Transformation
cards.body: Visualizing how data flows from unstructured documents into structured, searchable vector database entries.

left.text: Unstructured text files, PDFs, or API payloads with no systemized chunks or tags.

right.text: Uniform, metadata-tagged text chunks linked to mathematical vector embeddings.

exercises.prompt: Why do we chunk text before generating embeddings in the indexing pipeline?

options.text: To translate the text into multiple foreign languages automatically
To delete duplicate letters and punctuation marks
To fit within embedding model limits and keep search results precise
To encrypt the files so users cannot read them without a key

exercises.explain: Embedding models have token limits and perform better on focused, cohesive text snippets rather than giant documents.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Writing to the vector store happens at the very beginning of the indexing pipeline.
exercises.explain: Writing to the vector store is the final step in the pipeline, archiving the processed embeddings so they can be retrieved later.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the indexing pipeline.
exercises.sentence: After parsing and chunking, we enrich the text with metadata before embedding it.
exercises.explain: Adding metadata tags like author, source URL, or date during the pipeline makes it possible to filter search results later.

exercises.prompt: Identify the log output generated by a running indexing pipeline:

options.text: SQL query executed: SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = true; returned 150 rows.
Incoming GET request to /home from IP 192.168.1.1 - 200 OK.
[Pipeline] Parsed PDF -> Created 42 chunks -> Applied metadata tags -> Generated embeddings -> Written to vector store.

exercises.explain: Option C contains a realistic system log outlining the sequential pipeline stages: parsing, chunking, metadata enrichment, embedding generation, and writing to the database. Options A and B describe unrelated manual database queries or standard web server operations.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following sequences represents the correct order of stages in a standard production indexing pipeline?

options.text: Embed -> Chunk -> Parse -> Write to Store -> Enrich
Parse -> Chunk -> Enrich -> Embed -> Write to Store
Write to Store -> Embed -> Enrich -> Chunk -> Parse
Parse -> Embed -> Chunk -> Write to Store -> Enrich

exercises.explain: An indexing pipeline operates sequentially: raw data is parsed to extract text, divided into chunks, decorated with key metadata, converted into numerical vector embeddings, and finally saved to a vector database for quick search.
